authoritative_sources.title: Prior Authorization Process for Certain Hospital Outpatient Department (OPD) Services Frequently Asked Questions
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS publishes the controlling public operational guidance for Medicare outpatient prior-authorization workflows, including request submission routes, timeframes, affirmation or non-affirmation outcomes, and resubmission handling relevant to ambulatory prior-auth intake.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: ambulatory intake acceptance, decision timing, and resubmission control

authoritative_sources.title: Medicare Program Integrity Manual, Chapter 3 - Prior Authorization
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The CMS Program Integrity Manual is the governing Medicare administrative manual for prior-authorization request content, decision letters, and claim linkage, making it an authoritative operational source for intake evidence sufficiency and refusal boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: required request content and decision-ready evidence review

authoritative_sources.title: Documentation Requirement Lookup Service Initiative
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS's DRLS initiative is an official provider-facing documentation workflow resource that shows what supporting records are expected for services and therefore directly informs ambulatory prior-auth evidence preparation and retrieval behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: documentation lookup and evidence assembly before submission

authoritative_sources.title: Wasteful and Inappropriate Service Reduction (WISeR) Model Frequently Asked Questions
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS's WISeR FAQ is current public ambulatory and physician-services operating guidance describing required request facts, clinical-information expectations, and submission channels for selected outpatient services. It is a contemporary operational exemplar without being treated as universal payer policy.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: current ambulatory physician-service request intake and channel selection
used_for: ordering and servicing provider fact expectations
contemporary ambulatory terminology and system posture

authoritative_sources.title: CAQH CORE Operating Rules
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CAQH CORE is a recognized vendor-neutral operating-rules body used across payer and provider transactions. Its prior-authorization and attachment rules directly inform acknowledgements, connectivity, attachments, and error-handling expectations in prior-auth workflows.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: transaction acknowledgements, attachments, and error handling

authoritative_sources.title: Prior Authorization Resources
authoritative_sources.source_class: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: The AMA is the primary professional body for physician practices in the United States, and its prior-authorization resource hub is a recognized workflow reference for physician-office terminology, electronic prior-auth adoption, and workflow pain points that shape safe ambulatory queue behavior.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: physician-office prior-auth terminology and practice workflow framing

authoritative_sources.title: The Minimum Necessary Requirement
authoritative_sources.publisher: U.S. Department of Health & Human Services
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: HHS OCR's HIPAA minimum-necessary guidance is the controlling federal privacy standard for limiting PHI access and disclosure during authorization intake, attachment exchange, and outbound evidence packaging.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: privacy-gated evidence packaging and requester validation

authoritative_sources.title: CMS Interoperability and Prior Authorization Final Rule (CMS-0057-F)
authoritative_sources.source_class: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CMS-0057-F is the current federal control baseline for electronic prior-authorization exchange expectations, documentation transparency, and denial-reason communication for impacted payers, making it directly relevant to evidence completeness and systems integration boundaries.
authoritative_sources.workflow_stage: electronic prior-auth documentation transparency and response control

workflow_stages.name: request normalization and lane grounding
workflow_stages.description: Normalize the incoming ambulatory prior-auth request into patient, payer, provider, service, site-of-service, and urgency facts; verify that the work belongs to pre-service prior-authorization intake rather than scheduling, appeals, billing, or clinical review ownership.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: prerequisite and evidence validation
workflow_stages.description: Check identity, active coverage, referral or order status, service coding, diagnosis context, and required supporting documentation before the request can be accepted as ready for submission or downstream handling.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: authorization and access gating
workflow_stages.description: Determine whether authorization, referral, or other pre-service approvals are already present, pending, missing, or blocked and prevent scheduling or execution work from starting early.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

workflow_stages.name: transaction, attachment, and status reconciliation
workflow_stages.description: Use the authoritative work queue, portal, attachment, and documentation systems to reconcile request state, acknowledgements, and follow-up needs instead of guessing from partial records.
source_classes: Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane
Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane

workflow_stages.name: privacy, authority, and escalation handling
workflow_stages.description: Limit evidence to the minimum necessary, escalate unresolved disclosure or urgency claims, and route work that requires clinical judgment, payer adjudication, appeals handling, or post-service ownership.
source_classes: Evidence or control standards relevant to the lane
Recognized workflow references tied directly to the lane

artifacts: patient demographics and member eligibility response
ordering or referring provider record and NPI identifiers
requested CPT or HCPCS code set, ICD-10-CM diagnosis context, units, and place-of-service data
referral, order, authorization, or reference-number records
payer portal or clearinghouse acknowledgement and status history
supporting clinical documentation such as office notes, order, prior treatment history, imaging or test results, and payer questionnaires
minimum-necessary disclosure record, requester verification note, and escalation packet
intake disposition note, prerequisite checklist, and routing summary
systems_of_record: payer portal or electronic prior-authorization platform

allowed: Classify the request as accepted, pending, rejected, or routed based on documented patient, payer, provider, service, and evidence context.
Request or validate the minimum identity, eligibility, referral, authorization, and attachment facts needed to continue safely.
Prepare evidence-backed intake summaries, prerequisite checklists, and escalation packets without overstating approval authority.
Block pre-service handoff when referral, authorization, urgency basis, or evidence prerequisites are missing.
escalate: Required payer, tenant, jurisdiction, or site-of-service rules are missing and would change the answer.
Portal, practice-management, and EHR statuses materially conflict and the source-of-truth hierarchy is unresolved.
The request asserts expedited handling, medical necessity, privacy disclosure, or contract interpretation beyond the lane's authority.
Public baseline guidance and tenant or payer policy materially differ on required documentation, submission route, or approval dependencies.
refuse: Approve medical necessity, issue a payer determination, or override a denial.
Bypass referral, authorization, or eligibility checks to support scheduling or treatment progression.
Disclose more PHI than necessary or treat requester identity as assumed when disclosure authority is unresolved.
Take ownership of post-service retro-authorization, claims correction, legal appeal, or revenue-cycle resolution work.

domain_failure_modes: ambulatory work is scheduled or otherwise advanced before referral or authorization prerequisites are confirmed
generic intake handling drops CPT or HCPCS, ICD-10-CM, ordering NPI, site-of-service, or payer-portal context that materially changes the answer
clinical or payer-owned medical-necessity authority is wrongly inferred from possession of documentation
portal, attachment, and EHR status conflicts are guessed away rather than reconciled against authoritative systems
expedited handling is asserted without the required documented urgency basis
requesters receive more chart material than necessary because minimum-necessary and requester-verification controls are ignored
post-service billing or denial cleanup is misrouted into a pre-service prior-auth intake lane

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Boundary case that would overgeneralize from the industry name
Request that requires a more specialized adjacent lane

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: payer-specific prior-authorization requirement lists and attachment sets vary materially across product lines and states
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Can change whether prior authorization is required and what evidence is sufficient.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant and payer retrieval for the applicable plan, service line, and site of service before finalizing intake disposition.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: tenant source-of-truth precedence among payer portal, practice-management queue, and EHR referral shell may differ
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Can change which status is authoritative and whether the request is accepted, blocked, or reworked.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require tenant system-precedence mapping and escalate unresolved conflicts instead of inferring a final status.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: plan-specific expedited-review criteria and turnaround commitments differ beyond the public baseline
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Can change whether an urgency flag is valid and which escalation path applies.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Preserve the public baseline, require tenant or payer urgency policy, and treat missing criteria as escalation rather than approval.

notes: The lane is intentionally pre-service and pre-execution; it validates readiness and routes ownership shifts instead of making payer or clinician decisions.

Ambulatory and physician-service specificity is encoded through patient-access facts, coding, ordering context, site-of-service logic, payer portal status, and attachment discipline rather than generic call-center language.
No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains because each open ambiguity is converted into an explicit retrieval or escalation requirement for runtime use.
